story.intro: In 1640, Pierre de Fermat scribbled a startling claim in the margin of his copy of Arithmetica. It took mathematicians 358 years to prove. Today, you'll discover why it matters — not through a proof, but through play.
story.theoremStatement: If p is prime and a is any integer not divisible by p, then a^(p−1) ≡ 1 (mod p)
story.plainEnglish: When you multiply a number enough times and divide by a prime, the remainder is always 1 — as long as your starting number isn't already divisible by that prime.
applications: Netflix uses it to verify your password hasn't been stolen in a data breach
Your bank encrypts your card details using prime exponents — Fermat's theorem makes it possible
Mathematicians use it to test whether enormous numbers are prime in seconds

stages.question: What is 17 mod 5?
stages.hint: Divide 17 by 5. How many are left over? That's the remainder — which is what 'mod' gives you.

stages.question: Is 11 a prime number? (answer 'yes' or 'no')
stages.hint: A prime number has no divisors other than 1 and itself. Does 11 divide evenly by any number besides 1 and 11?
stages.conceptShown: 11 is prime — it has no divisors other than 1 and itself

stages.question: If p = 7 (prime) and a = 3, what is 3^(7−1) mod 7 — that is, 3^6 mod 7?
stages.hint: Calculate 3^6 = 729. Now divide 729 by 7. What's the remainder?

stages.question: If p = 11 and a = 5, what is 5^(11−1) mod 11, or 5^10 mod 11?
stages.hint: Since 5 is not divisible by 11 (the prime), Fermat's Little Theorem tells us the answer is 1. No complex calculation needed.
stages.conceptShown: 5^10 mod 11 = 1 — Fermat's Little Theorem at work

stages.question: What is 2^10 mod 11?
stages.hint: Again apply Fermat's Little Theorem. 2 is not divisible by 11, p = 11, so a^(p-1) = 2^10 ≡ 1 (mod 11).

stages.question: What is 7^12 mod 13? (hint: a^(p−1) ≡ 1 mod p when p is prime and a is not divisible by p)
stages.hint: 7 and 13 are coprime (7 is not divisible by 13). p = 13, so 7^12 ≡ 1 (mod 13).

stages.question: Final challenge: For any prime p and any a not divisible by p, a^(p−1) mod p equals...?
stages.hint: That's the whole point of Fermat's Little Theorem. The answer is 1 — it's always 1.
stages.conceptShown: a^(p−1) ≡ 1 (mod p) — Fermat's Little Theorem, stated formally
